title: Stable Subarray Reversal Index Validation after Two Shift Operations

statement.markdown:
Given an array of integers, implement a function to check if the array is valid after two consecutive shift operations. A shift operation is defined as reversing a subarray of the given array. The function should return true if the array is valid after two consecutive shifts and false otherwise.
Example: Input arr = [5, 3, 4, 5, 2, 3], Output: True
Note: The function should work for any size of input array.
statement.inputFormat: Input is an array of integers
statement.outputFormat: Boolean: True if array is valid after two consecutive shifts, False otherwise
statement.notes: Shift operations can be done in any order, and a subarray can be reversed in any order.

examples.explanation: After shifting [2, 3] to the start and [4, 5] to the end, we get [3, 4, 5, 5, 3, 2] which is the reversed subarray

hints.text: Check if array is valid after one shift operation

editorial.title: Solution Explanation
editorial.intuition:
The problem can be solved by checking if array is valid after one shift operation. A shift operation is valid if there exists a subarray such that its reverse is equal to the remaining array.
First, check if array has odd length if array has odd length, then return True. Otherwise, initialize two pointers one at each end of the array, and iterate the array in a two-pointer technique. If the two pointers meet in the middle, then return True. Otherwise, return False.
editorial.bruteForce: Brute force approach is to check all possible subarrays and check if their reverse is equal to the remaining array.
editorial.betterSolution: Use two-pointer technique to check if array has a valid shift operation.
editorial.optimalSolution: Check if array has odd length and if so, then return True. Otherwise, check if any subarray exists such that its reverse is equal to the remaining array.
editorial.proofOfCorrectness: By construction of the two-pointer technique, we know that if the array has a valid shift operation, then the two pointers will meet in the middle
editorial.dryRun: Check if the array has odd length, then return True. Otherwise, initialize two pointers at each end of the array and iterate the array in a two-pointer technique.
commonMistakes: Not checking if array has odd length
